coredata.dc:title: Coated proppants with self-suspension and tracer slow-release functions
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Petroleum Science and Engineering
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Yang
Liu, Hao
Gao, Jiajia
Fan, Fan
Niu, Yingchun
Li, Shouzhen
Mi, Yuanyuan
Chen, Siyuan
Xu, Quan
coredata.dc:description: Proppants play an important role to increase oil and gas output in shale oil and gas development process. Tracers are used to study the degree of heterogeneity and dynamic equivalence between injection and production wells to judge the state of oil reservoir. However, it is difficult to integrate both slow-release tracing and shale supporting functions in one material. The development of functional coated proppants with tracer slow-release function is of great significance. Here a simple method is developed to introduce a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) coated proppants and fabricate novel self-suspension proppants with tracer slow-release function. The PVA coating layer on ceramic proppants achieves the self-suspension ability. In the well, the PVA film melted in fracturing fluid and the CQDs were released as a tracer for downhole monitoring. The surface adhesion force of PVA-CQDs coated proppants is 2 times larger than that of ordinary ceramic proppants, and the liquid conductivity of the self-suspension slow-release tracer proppants (SSTP) can reach 51 D cm, which is 22.45% higher than original ceramic proppants at the closure pressure of 40 MPa. This work combined the coated proppants with the quantum dot tracer and obtained a new type of coated proppants with tracer slow-release function, which has a promising significance to improve oil and gas production and conducting cross-well monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Self-suspension
Slow-release tracer proppants
Quantum dot
Polyvinyl alcohol

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Yang Zhou and Hao Liu contributed equally to this work. This research was supported by Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (Nos. 2462019BJRC007 , 2462019QNXZ02 ), National Key Research and Development Plan ( No：SQ2020YFC190006-02 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of proppants coating; (b) Schematic diagram of the slow-release process of proppants.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) photographs of the surface morphology of ceramic proppants and SSTP. (a) (c) Macro image of uncoated proppants and SSTP; (b) (d) Microscopic image of uncoated proppants and SSTP.

EDX mapping of the uncoated proppants and SSTP. (a) (f)SEM image of the uncoated proppants and SSTP; (b) (g) Carbon on the surface of the uncoated proppants and SSTP; (c) (h) Silicon on the surface of the uncoated proppants and SSTP; (d) (i) Aluminum on the surface of the uncoated proppants and SSTP; (e) (j) Niobium on the surface of the uncoated proppants and SSTP.

(a) Adhesion force changes of SSTP and uncoated proppants under different load force; (b) Adhesion force changes of SSTP and uncoated proppants under different contact time; (c) Typical AFM force curve in adhesion measurement for a load force of 1.5 μN and a contact time of 1.5 s; (d) Liquid conductivity of the SSTP and uncoated proppants under different closure pressure; (e) Fluorescence intensity change of SSTP with time at 95 °C; (f) Fluorescence intensity change of SSTP with time at room temperature.
Self-suspension experiment results and pictures of SSTP and uncoated proppants under different guar gum concentrations.
The slow-release process of SSTP at (a) room temperature; (b) 90 °C.

head.ce:title: Coated proppants with self-suspension and tracer slow-release functions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Petroleum Resources and Prospecting, Beijing Key Laboratory of Biogas Upgrading Utilization, China University of Petroleum (Beijing), Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Petroleum Resources and Prospecting
Beijing Key Laboratory of Biogas Upgrading Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Petroleum Resources and Prospecting, Beijing Key Laboratory of Biogas Upgrading Utilization, China University of Petroleum (Beijing), Beijing 102249, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Proppants play an important role to increase oil and gas output in shale oil and gas development process. Tracers are used to study the degree of heterogeneity and dynamic equivalence between injection and production wells to judge the state of oil reservoir. However, it is difficult to integrate both slow-release tracing and shale supporting functions in one material. The development of functional coated proppants with tracer slow-release function is of great significance. Here a simple method is developed to introduce a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) coated proppants and fabricate novel self-suspension proppants with tracer slow-release function. The PVA coating layer on ceramic proppants achieves the self-suspension ability. In the well, the PVA film melted in fracturing fluid and the CQDs were released as a tracer for downhole monitoring. The surface adhesion force of PVA-CQDs coated proppants is 2 times larger than that of ordinary ceramic proppants, and the liquid conductivity of the self-suspension slow-release tracer proppants (SSTP) can reach 51 D cm, which is 22.45% higher than original ceramic proppants at the closure pressure of 40 MPa. This work combined the coated proppants with the quantum dot tracer and obtained a new type of coated proppants with tracer slow-release function, which has a promising significance to improve oil and gas production and conducting cross-well monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: In recently, the demand of oil and natural gas has been sharply risen under the rapidly increasing world economic development.(Li et al., 2019; C. Xu et al., 2018). However, the production capacity of oil and natural gas is relatively weak, which seriously limits the development of all industries (Halabi et al., 2015; Qijun et al., 2018; Fangzheng, 2019). Therefore, the efficient exploitation of unconventional oil and gas is of great significance to solve the current shortage of oil and gas resources(Webb et al., 2018; Xiaoguang et al., 2018; L. Zheng et al., 2017).

In China, unconventional oil and gas reserves are 2.23 × 1010–2.63 × 1010 t and 8.4 × 1013–1.25 × 1014 m3 respectively. Unconventional oil and gas reservoirs are complex in structure, which is difficult to be exploited(Chengzao, 2017; Hecobian et al., 2019). Proppants play an important and indispensable role in unconventional oil and gas exploitation.

At present, the common proppants include quartz sand, ceramic proppants, and resin-coated proppants(Liang et al., 2016; Michael et al., 2020; Zoveidavianpoor and Gharibi, 2015). Quartz sand is widely used in wells with low closure pressure, but it begins to fracture at about 20 MPa due to its low compressive strength(Zou et al., 2018), its low sphericity will cause a lot of wear on the drilling equipment(Zheng and Costa, 2018). Compared with quartz sand, ceramic proppants has higher strength and resistance to breakage, but it has the disadvantages of high density, large cost and big construction risk(Wen et al., 2016). Moreover, resin-coated proppants take advantage of higher sphericity and lower density, which can prevent the proppants reflow. The superiority of resin-coated proppants is low density, strong pressure resistance and prevent embedding(Quan et al., 2020). At the same time, it is also suitable for formations with high closure pressure to achieve better engineering application effects(Cao et al., 2020a; Carpenter, 2016; Haque et al., 2019). In addition, new proppants including low-density proppants(Hao et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2015), self-suspending proppants(Gol et al., 2017), and non-spherical proppants(J. Xu et al., 2020) have also been gradually developed. Due to its low density and high strength, self-suspending proppants can migrate to the far end of fracture together with the fracturing fluid, which makes proppants more widely distributed and better support the rock fractures, thereby increasing the liquid conductivity(Goldstein and VanZeeland, 2015; Kincaid et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2017). Therefore, the usage of self-suspending proppants in unconventional oil and gas production is gradually increasing.

During the development of oil and gas wells, the downhole stratum structure is intricate and may produce thin layers with extremely high permeability. Due to the long-term erosion of injected water, the internal structure and physical parameters of the reservoir will change, and the tracer test is by injecting the tracer into the well, and then testing the output tracer from surrounding production wells (Al-Qasim et al., 2020; Morozov et al., 2017; Sanni et al., 2017). As one of the effective monitoring methods, tracer technology be used to describe the reservoir clearly and reduce the difficulty of field operations. The most commonly used water tracer in oilfields are the fluorobenzoic acid compounds (FBAs) and the gas tracer are the perfluorocarbons.(Tayyib et al., 2019). And other common chemical tracers include: (1) ionic type, such as SCN−, NO3 −, Br−, I−, etc. (2) organic type, such as formaldehyde, ethanol, isopropanol, etc. Tritium radioactive material such as butanol and tritiated ethanol are often used as tracers between wells. These commonly used tracers are usually easy to damage the reservoir formation, where radioactive tracers are more harmful. As a new type of tracer, carbon dots have the advantages of chemical and biological stability, wide sources, low cost, and non-toxicity, which have the potential to replace radioactive tracers.

In this work, we prepared a kind of PVA-CQDs coated self-suspension proppants with tracer slow-release function. The coated proppants have a novelty self-suspension performance, which is 3 times higher than uncoated ceramic proppants. Compared with uncoated ceramic proppants, the liquid conductivity of the slow-release tracer proppants (SSTP) is enhanced by 22.45%. At the same time, the PVA material increased the adhesion force between proppants and the rock fracture, thereby achieving improved diversion capacity. Besides, PVA-CQDs also successfully achieved slow-release in aqueous solvents. After 2 h of slow-release, the maximum fluorescence intensity can reach 1.8 × 105, which can achieve the best slow-release effect after proppants are pumped into the well. The combination of proppants and the slow-release agent gives SSTP a new function that can reduce oil and gas well development costs to a certain extent. This idea has a guiding role in the design and development of new proppants and has a great significance to improve the efficiency of unconventional oil and gas production.

Nitric acid and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. Polyvinyl alcohol and L-cysteine were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Reagents used in the experiments were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water is produced by a water deionizer (model BK-10B, Dongguanshi Qianjing environmental equipment Co., Ltd). The proppants used in the experiment are common proppants on the market.

First 100 mg of Nb2C Mxene material was mixed with concentrated nitric acid and concentrated sulfuric acid, react at 100 °C for 12 h in oil, then cooling to room temperature, sodium hydroxide solution was added to neutralize the pH to 7, then the functionalized Nb2C Mxene-based quantum dots doped with nitrogen（N-MQDs） are prepared by hydrothermal method. The supernatant of the product prepared in the first step is put into polytetrafluoroethylene together with 20 ml deionized water and 0.05 g L-cysteine. In a vinyl fluoride liner, react at 160 °C for 12 h. In the third step, the hydrothermal product is evaporated to dryness with a Rotary evaporator. Obtained solid nitrogen-doped functional Nb2C Mxene-based quantum dots.

500 mg of N-MQDs, 2 g PVA, and 30 ml of deionized water were mixed and heated at 98 °C for 1 h. When it becomes molten, mix the proppants and the liquid thoroughly. The solid-liquid mixture was then sonicated for 20 min in an ultrasonic cleaner. The mixture is uniformly dispersed and dried in a thermostat at 50 °C for 8 h. Finally, the dried product is taken out and the supports that partially agglomerate together are separated to obtain coated proppants with quantum dot slow-release function.

Full-diameter core acid erosion conductivity evaluation device were used to test fracture liquid conductivity. The rock used in the experiment is granite. The porosity of granite is 0.3%∼0.7%, the water absorption is 0.13%∼0.3%, and the compressive strength is 150 MPa.

First, 0.2% guar gum solution was prepared and stir at 500 r/min. Then slowly add 5 g of proppants to the mixed solution and stir for 3 min. Collect the floating proppants and the bottom proppants and proceed separately After drying, the self-suspension efficiency of the proppants can be calculated by the following formula:
(1)
w t
% =
m 1
/
(
m 1
+
m 2
)
× 100 %
Where m
1 is the weight of the floated proppants and the m
2 is that of the Sunken proppants.

Zeiss Sigma 500 and Bruker XFlash 6/30 were used to capture the scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) images of the sample. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was performed on a Brukerdimension Ion with Scan Asyst. FCS-842 was used to measure the liquid conductivity of proppants. Fluorescence measurements were performed with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FS5).

Fig. 1 a shows the schematic design of the slow-release tracer proppants (SSTP), carbon dots (CDs) doped polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) coated on ceramic proppants. Fig. 1b illustrates the slow-release mechanism of the tracer. During the fracturing process, SSTP enter the downhole with the fracturing fluid. Then the volume of PVA film will increase and channels between the polymer chains are generated. The CDs embedded between polymer chains start to release along the formed channel. Finally, the PVA layer will dissolve and all the CDs will be released in water.

Fig. 2 a and b are scanning electron microscope (SEM) photographs of the surface morphology of ceramic proppants. Potholes and irregular protrusions on the rough surface can be observed on ceramic proppants surfaces. SEM photographs of the SSTP are shown in Fig. 2c and d. Compared with uncoated proppants, the PVA coating on the surface of the SSTP makes the originally rough surface smoother. There are fewer pits and irregular protrusions on the surface of the SSTP compared with uncoated proppants. Moreover, Fig. 2d shows the micro-topography of the SSTP's surface, the surface of the SSTP is tidier than uncoated proppants (Fig. 2b).

The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) mapping test was conducted of both uncoated proppants and SSTP to further prove that PVA was successfully coated on the surface of ceramic proppants. As shown in the EDX result of uncoated proppants (Fig. 3 a) and SSTP (Fig. 3b), carbon element is unable to be detected on the surface of the uncoated proppants. For SSTP, where carbon element can be seen on the surface of SSTP indicated PVA layer has been attached on the ceramic proppants. As shown in Fig. 3c and d and Fig. 3h and i, silicon (Fig. 3c) and aluminum (Fig. 3d) elements can be observed on the surface of the uncoated proppants. On the contrary, silicon (Fig. 3h) and aluminum (Fig. 3i) elements were almost undetectable on the surface of SSTP, which proved PVA layer completely covered the ceramic proppants. Moreover, the labeled niobium element contained in the CDs was detected (Fig. 3j), which proved traces have been embedded inside PVA coating layer successfully. In summary, all the above proved that we have coated the surface of the proppants with a PVA film containing CDs successfully.

The adhesive ability of SSTP was conducted by atomic force microscopy (AFM). Fig. 4 a shows the variations of adhesion forces under different load force conditions, and the contact time was fixed at 0.1 s. With the load force increased from 0.5 μN to 3 μN, the adhesion force of SSTP is larger than uncoated proppants in each testing load force, which is 213.91, 186.41, 181.22, 163.04, 177.78, 148.81% higher, respectively. With the increase of the load force, the adhesion of the coated and uncoated proppants increased slightly. But under the same conditions, the adhesion force of the SSTP is higher than that of the uncoated proppants. The contact time versus adhesion force of the proppants is shown in Fig. 4b, where the contact time increased from 0.5 to 2.0 s, and the load force was kept as 1.5 μN. When the contact time was changed, there was no significant change in the adhesion force between the SSTP and uncoated proppants. The adhesion forces of coated proppants changed by 215.53, 165.89, 238.63, 239.47, 276.50%, compared with the uncoated proppants, respectively. The typical AFM force (known as force curve) is illustrated in Fig. 4c, compared with uncoated proppants, SSTP have a higher adhesion force. Fig. 4d displays the liquid conductivity of SSTP and uncoated proppants under different effective closure stress changed from 5 MPa to 50 MPa, with the increase of the closing pressure, the liquid conductivity of both proppants decreased, but the liquid conductivity of SSTP is higher than that of uncoated proppants. This may be due to the presence of PVA on the surface of SSTP, which makes SSTP can be exposed to water.

The fluorescence intensity of a solution containing carbon quanta can directly reflect the concentration of CDs in the solution. Fig. 4f shows the fluorescence intensity changed at room temperature, the fluorescence intensity is 7.5 × 104 at the beginning, after 90 min, the fluorescence intensity increased to 12 × 104, the calculated slow-release rate is ∼500/min. In order to simulate the downhole environment, SSTP were dispersed in water at 95 °C with 600 rpm/h stirring rate. The fluorescence intensity of the solution was 1.07 × 105 at the beginning, which increased to 1.8 × 105 after 90 min. The calculated slow-release rate of SSTP in water at 95 °C is ∼811/min, which is 62.2% higher than SSTP in water at room temperature (Fig. 4e). Due to constant heating and stirring, the PVA film on the surface of the SSTP swelled and the CDs were released continuously. With the release of the CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the solution gradually increased. which also shows that temperature will accelerate the slow-release effect.

Fig. 5 shows the histogram of the self-suspension capacity of SSTP and uncoated proppants. The weight of the proppants floating on the solution surface is various with the change of the concentration of the guar gum solution, they are 0.27, 0.26, 0.29, 0.32, 0.315 g, respectively. For uncoated proppants, they are 0.05, 0.05, 0.10, 0.10, 0.12 g, respectively. Compared with the uncoated proppants, the self-suspension capacity of SSTP is about 4 times of the uncoated proppants. The reason may be that the coated low-density shell on the surface of the ceramic proppants reduces SSTP's density and gives SSTP a low-density characteristic.

In order to further demonstrate the influence of temperature on the slow-release effect of SSTP, the color change of the solution after different times at room temperature and 90 °C were recorded. Fig. 6 a shows the slow-release process at room temperature, the color of the solution changes very slightly, a slight color change can be observed around the SSTP after 600 s. Compared with the slow-release process under room temperature, the slow-release process at 90 °C can be observed clearly. The change of the color is obvious that the entire solution has changed at 600 s. The above results further prove that heating can accelerate the release of slow-release agents.

In summary, we successfully prepared PVA coated proppants with a quantum dot slow-release function, which has a core-shell structure. This work puts forward a new design idea for functional proppants, which is expected to be successfully applied to shale oil and gas field mining in the next few years. The main conclusions of this paper are listed as follows.
(1)
The surface adhesion force of PVA-CQDs coated proppants is 2 times larger than that of ordinary ceramic proppants, and the liquid conductivity of the self-suspension slow-release tracer proppants (SSTP) can reach 51 D cm, which is 22.45% higher than original ceramic proppants at the closure pressure of 40 MPa.
(2)
When the guar gum concentration is 0.2%, the self-suspension ability of SSTP is about 4 times that of the uncoated proppants. The better self-suspension ability allows the proppants to move to a longer distance while reducing the use of proppants, it can also achieve a better support effect and improve the support efficiency.
(3)
The slow-release functions has been achieved due to the exist of QCDs in SSTP. At 90 °C, the quantum dot tracer can be released within 120 min, where the maximum fluorescence intensity can reach 1.8 × 105.

In this manuscript, Quan Xu concerned the idea and supervised this manuscript. Yang Zhou and Hao Liu operated most of the experiments and analysis the result. Jiajia Aao operated the SEM test, Fanfan operated the AFM test, Yingchun Niu helped with the result analysis, Shouzeng Li and Yuanyuan Mi prepared the carbon dots, Siyuan Chen helped with the liquid conductivity test. All the authors joined the writing and revision process.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Yang Zhou and Hao Liu contributed equally to this work. This research was supported by Science Foundation of China University of Petroleum, Beijing (Nos. 2462019BJRC007, 2462019QNXZ02), National Key Research and Development Plan (No：SQ2020YFC190006-02).
